text: An 18-day taper following 40 days of high-volume sprint interval training improves short-duration exercise capacity (repeated runs at 90% vVO2max) by ~21% compared to pre-training values in moderately trained runners.

Tapering for 18 days after high-volume sprint interval training improves 10-km performance by 2.7% compared to the end of the high-volume phase, but does not significantly improve it beyond pre-training levels.

A taper structure of reducing sprint interval sessions from 20 sessions over 40 days to 4 sessions over 18 days (with reduced volume per session from 8-12 to 4 repetitions) effectively restores and improves performance.
specific_value: 20 sessions/40 days reduced to 4 sessions/18 days

text: Running economy (oxygen uptake at 60% vVO2max) improves after an 18-day taper from high-volume sprint interval training compared to both pre-training and post-high-volume values.
Muscular adaptations from high-volume sprint interval training (increased Na+/K+ ATPase and SERCA1 expression) are retained through an 18-day taper despite large reductions in training volume.

The optimal taper duration should be matched to the target competition distance — tapering from sprint interval training benefits short-duration performance more than longer-distance (10-km) performance.
10-km performance improvements from tapering appear to plateau midway through an 18-day taper, with no additional gains in the second half of the taper period.
specific_value: 40.7 min at both midway and end of taper
